 succeeded in 0ms:
   120		return v.Index.Search(query, limit)
   121	}
   122	
   123	// Status returns vault-wide statistics.
   124	func (v *Vault) Status() map[string]interface{} {
   125		counts := make(map[string]int)
   126		filepath.Walk(v.Dir, func(path string, info os.FileInfo, err error) error {
   127			if err != nil || info.IsDir() || !strings.HasSuffix(path, ".md") {
   128				return nil
   129			}
   130			rel, _ := filepath.Rel(v.Dir, path)
   131			parts := strings.SplitN(rel, string(os.PathSeparator), 2)
   132			counts[parts[0]]++
   133			return nil
   134		})
   135	
   136		total := 0
   137		for _, count := range counts {
   138			total += count
   139		}
   140	
   141		indexDocs := 0
   142		if v.Index != nil {
   143			indexDocs = v.Index.DocCount()
   144		}
   145	
   146		result := map[string]interface{}{
   147			"total_files": total,
   148			"index_docs":  indexDocs,
   149			"breakdown":   counts,
   150		}
   151		return result
   152	}
   153	
   154	// StatusJSON returns Status() as formatted JSON.
   155	func (v *Vault) StatusJSON() (string, error) {
   156		data, err := json.MarshalIndent(v.Status(), "", "  ")
   157		return string(data), err
   158	}

 succeeded in 0ms:
   454	<summary><strong>Can I use it with multiple AI clients at the same time?</strong></summary>
   455	
   456	Yes. Each client spawns its own modus-memory process, but they all read/write the same vault directory. The index is rebuilt on startup (takes ~2 seconds), and writes go directly to disk as markdown files. There's no lock contention for reads.
   457	</details>
   458	
   459	<details>
   460	<summary><strong>What happens if I have 100K+ documents?</strong></summary>
   461	
   462	The index is pure in-memory Go. Startup time scales roughly linearly — expect ~10 seconds for 100K docs on Apple Silicon. Search time stays constant (cache hit) or sub-10ms (cold). The binary's memory usage is roughly 2x the total text size of your vault.
   463	</details>
   464	
   465	<details>
   466	<summary><strong>Can I encrypt my vault?</strong></summary>
   467	
   468	modus-memory reads whatever filesystem you give it. Use OS-level encryption:

## Task 7: Verify and harden FTS5 ablation integrity (alignment guard + evaluation mode + parent-id normalization)

### rollout_summary_files

- rollout_summaries/2026-03-28T09-47-16-BtZ6-ablation_benchmark_integrity.md (cwd=/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Opencode Env/Public, rollout_path=/Users/michelkerkmeester/.codex/sessions/2026/03/28/rollout-2026-03-28T10-47-16-019d33d7-38dc-7343-8b10-d28cc9573e58.jsonl, updated_at=2026-03-28T10:51:34+00:00, thread_id=019d33d7-38dc-7343-8b10-d28cc9573e58)
- rollout_summaries/2026-03-28T08-25-17-MKwU-spec_kit_ablation_verif_docs_2026_03_28.md (cwd=/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Opencode Env/Public, rollout_path=/Users/michelkerkmeester/.codex/sessions/2026/03/28/rollout-2026-03-28T09-25-17-019d338c-2aa0-7a83-b30f-2ddefd76e320.jsonl, updated_at=2026-03-28T09:44:06+00:00, thread_id=019d338c-2aa0-7a83-b30f-2ddefd76e320, partial outcome)

### keywords

- eval_run_ablation, assertGroundTruthAlignment, evaluationMode, parentMemoryId ?? row.id, Token budget overflow, contamination_audit, scripts/evals/map-ground-truth-ids.ts, context-index__voyage__voyage-4__1024.sqlite

## User preferences

- user required “log warning and continue instead of returning rejected” for warn-only Gates 1-3 and “do not modify ... memory-index.ts” -> keep scope tightly in `processPreparedMemory()` [Task 1]
- user required build + runtime verification (`npm run build`, forced scan/index evidence) before closeout [Task 1]
- when user asked “how to fix whilst keeping auto,” keep `EMBEDDINGS_PROVIDER=auto` and avoid hardcoded DB/env paths that force provider fallback [Task 4]
- when Desktop MCP was missing and user asked “add to user level codex config first,” apply `~/.codex/config.toml` feature/config edits before deeper probes [Task 6]
- when user then said “just tried but no luck. investigate deeper,” continue beyond one fix attempt into session metadata/log evidence [Task 6]
- when user requested ablation verification, run `eval_run_ablation({ mode:"ablation", includeFormattedReport:true, storeResults:true })` through Codex MCP before declaring FTS5 outcomes [Task 7]
- when user ordered ablation first and docs second, resolve Codex↔spec_kit_memory handshake blockers before documentation edits [Task 7]

## Reusable knowledge

- Warn-only pattern: Gates 1-3 should log and continue for warn-only spec docs; enforce mode must preserve rejection payloads [Task 1]
- Run scan tooling from repo root so base path resolves actual spec folders; account for 60-second scan cooldown [Task 1]
- Ground-truth remap uses read-only DB scan + atomic JSON write + hard_negative skip + dedupe by `file_path::title` [Task 2]
- Set `MEMORY_DB_PATH` consistently across client config surfaces to writable home path [Task 3]
- With `EMBEDDINGS_PROVIDER=auto`, forward `VOYAGE_API_KEY` and `OPENAI_API_KEY` into the MCP child process and avoid forcing a single shared DB file that can cross provider dimensions [Task 4]
- If `list_mcp_resources`/`list_mcp_resource_templates` are empty, treat `spec_kit_memory` as configured-but-not-exposed and verify config + handler presence before assuming tool absence in code [Task 5]
- Desktop `session_meta.dynamic_tools` plus absence of `mcp_startup` events is a stronger surfacing signal than standalone `node context-server.js` timeout probes [Task 6]
- Use `codex features enable memories` and `codex features enable code_mode`, then start a new Desktop thread to test MCP surfacing; gate toggles persist in `~/.codex/config.toml` [Task 6]
- When startup background scan emits `contamination_audit` JSON on stdout, Codex MCP JSON-RPC can fail to handshake; redirect/silence that stream or delay MCP client init until scan completion [Task 7]
- Treat any ablation run showing `Token budget overflow` and returning fewer than `recallK` results as investigation-only, not a reliable Recall@20 verdict [Task 7]

codex
# Iteration 028: BENCHMARK & METRICS

## Focus
BENCHMARK & METRICS: What does this system measure? What metrics should we track for memory quality, retrieval accuracy, and system health?

## Findings
### Finding 1: Modus exposes operational health counts, not real retrieval-quality benchmarks
- **Source**: [vault.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/vault/vault.go#L123), [main.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/cmd/modus-memory/main.go#L139), [doctor.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/cmd/modus-memory/doctor.go#L26)
- **What it does**: Modus surfaces `total_files`, `index_docs`, per-folder breakdown, fact totals/active counts, cross-ref cardinality, plus integrity checks like missing subject/predicate, duplicate subject+predicate pairs, contradictions, empty docs, and frontmatter gaps.
- **Why it matters**: For our system, this means health should be a separate panel from retrieval evaluation. Modus’s operator story is “is the vault sane and indexable?”, not “is retrieval objectively good?”
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 2: Modus has useful internal retrieval signals, but almost no first-class benchmark telemetry
- **Source**: [bm25.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/index/bm25.go#L14), [cache.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/index/cache.go#L13), [indexer.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/index/indexer.go#L169), [search.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/librarian/search.go#L15), [README.md](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/README.md#L462)
- **What it does**: The code has field weights, normalized ranks, Jaccard cache reuse, OOD filtering, and query-expansion fan-out, but it does not persist cache-hit rate, expansion efficacy, latency histograms, or per-query accuracy outcomes. The performance story is mostly comments and README copy.
- **Why it matters**: We should not treat Modus’s current “sub-10ms cold” or “~10 seconds at 100K docs” claims as benchmark truth. The transferable part is which signals exist, not the published numbers.
- **Recommendation**: reject
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 3: Modus’s strongest memory-quality metrics are lifecycle metrics, not usefulness metrics
- **Source**: [facts.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/vault/facts.go#L18), [facts.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/vault/facts.go#L160), [index-facts.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/index/facts.go#L53), [mcp-vault.go](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/external/internal/mcp/vault.go#L311)
- **What it does**: Modus tracks `confidence`, `stability`, `difficulty`, importance floors, recency tier, `last_accessed`, and `access_count`, then auto-reinforces on `memory_search`.
- **Why it matters**: The right memory-quality dashboard for us should include retrievability distribution, stability growth, difficulty drift, floor saturation, archived share, explicit-review rate vs access-review rate, and access-count concentration. Those are lifecycle quality signals that usefulness-only feedback misses.
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 4: Spec Kit Memory already has the stronger offline retrieval benchmark stack
- **Source**: [eval-metrics.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/eval/eval-metrics.ts#L48), [eval-metrics.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/eval/eval-metrics.ts#L179), [ablation-framework.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/eval/ablation-framework.ts#L145), [reporting-dashboard.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/eval/reporting-dashboard.ts#L52), [shadow-scoring.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/feedback/shadow-scoring.ts#L55)
- **What it does**: Public already computes MRR, NDCG, Recall, Precision, F1, MAP, Hit Rate, inversion rate, constitutional surfacing, importance-weighted recall, cold-start detection, channel ablation deltas, holdout shadow deltas, and dashboard trends.
- **Why it matters**: The benchmark backbone should stay in the eval DB we already have. The missing work is not “invent benchmark math,” but “extend the existing eval system with memory-lifecycle and operability metrics.”
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 5: Spec Kit Memory already records strong online/system telemetry; the gap is making it operator-visible
- **Source**: [retrieval-telemetry.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/telemetry/retrieval-telemetry.ts#L52), [retrieval-telemetry.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/telemetry/retrieval-telemetry.ts#L76), [eval-quality-proxy.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/eval/eval-quality-proxy.ts#L8), [stage2-fusion.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/pipeline/stage2-fusion.ts#L923), [context-server.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts#L1652)
- **What it does**: Public already tracks stage latencies, mode selection, fallbacks, quality proxy score, graph-injection counts, adaptive deltas, quality-filter counts, and startup health like `memory_index` vs `active_memory_projection`.
- **Why it matters**: The benchmarkable system-health set is mostly already here. What we need is a compact operator surface that reports p50/p95 latency, fallback rate, route selection, graph contribution rate, projection drift, and degraded-run counts without needing trace spelunking.
- **Recommendation**: NEW FEATURE
- **Impact**: high

### Finding 6: Public also has the missing “memory usefulness” layer that Modus largely lacks
- **Source**: [confidence-tracker.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/scoring/confidence-tracker.ts#L96), [checkpoints.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/checkpoints.ts#L648), [memory-crud-stats.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-stats.ts#L205), [quality-loop.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/quality-loop.ts#L690), [memory-crud-health.ts](/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-health.ts#L296)
- **What it does**: Public tracks `confidence`, `validation_count`, positive/negative validation flows, promotion eligibility, folder validation signals, `memory_quality_score`, alias divergence, and embedding retry health.
- **Why it matters**: The right quality scorecard for us should combine four groups: usefulness (`wasUseful`, promotion eligibility, negative-feedback rate), content quality (`quality_score`, memory_quality_score), integrity (contradiction/alias/duplicate counts), and infra health (embedding retry backlog, pending reindex, projection drift).
- **Recommendation**: adopt now
- **Impact**: high

## Assessment
- New information ratio: 0.46
- Modus measures mostly three buckets: vault integrity counts, retrieval-internal heuristics, and FSRS lifecycle state. It does not provide a serious benchmark harness for retrieval quality.
- Public already has the better benchmark substrate. The practical metric plan is:
  1. Retrieval accuracy: `Recall@20`, `MRR@5`, `NDCG@10`, `Precision@5/20`, `MAP`, `Hit Rate`, channel ablation delta, holdout shadow delta.
  2. Memory quality: `confidence`, `validation_count`, positive usefulness rate, negative-feedback rate, `quality_score`, `memory_quality_score`, retrievability/stability/difficulty distributions, archive rate.
  3. System health: p50/p95 total latency, per-stage latency, fallback rate, route/mode selection frequency, cache-hit/fuzzy-hit rate, OOD reject rate, expansion fan-out, graph-injection rate, startup projection drift, embedding retry backlog, alias/contradiction counts.
- Strict validation returned `RESULT: PASSED` for the phase folder, with the same read-only warning: `cannot create temp file for here document: Operation not permitted`.
- This was a read-only iteration; I verified source evidence and phase validation, but did not update phase artifacts.

## Recommended Next Focus
Design one concrete measurement contract for Public with three surfaces only: `offline_eval`, `online_proxy`, and `memory_lifecycle`. The next useful iteration is to map each metric above to its existing source of truth, identify the missing counters (`cache-hit`, `OOD reject`, `expansion fan-out`, `auto-review vs explicit-review`), and define the minimal dashboard/CLI output that makes routing and health observable without full trace output.
